item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That twist hit like a brick. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to imagine that the ethics committee has already figured out who Dr. Lane really is and that's why they approved of the changes to containment; they don't need to interrogate or test him anymore because they now have a loyal and cooperative Foundation employee who has undergone the same immortality ritual, and also he's a terrible dangerous person who kind of deserves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking Bethany set up the murder so that the Ethics Committee would approve her proposal more readily. The mind break was just the cherry on top. No way are they gonna approve of immobilizing an immortal for all eternity otherwise.

item.title: Re: Wait a minute, how'd this happen we're smarter than this?
item.content:encoded: Ahhh, okay. I think I got it now, Thank you

item.title: Re: Wait a minute, how'd this happen we're smarter than this?
item.content:encoded:
You've actually gotten some details there completely incorrect. That said, when this article was written some of the dates in question were in the future. This is a snapshot of the documentation just after they dug up the grave.

You're absolutely correct that the Foundation now has enough information to move on, but at the time of the documentation they have not actually had time to move on anything.

item.title: Wait a minute, how'd this happen we're smarter than this?
item.content:encoded:
+ SPOILERS
- SPOILERS

I feel like this should include an addendum or “security alert” saying that Agent Cassidy (Bethany Manfred) has disappeared and that she is a medium/high priority to locate and capture her due to her connection with SCP-2632 (possibly redesignste her as SCP-2632-2) and her knowledge of the Foundation, also possibly before it have a notice of the Ethics Committee starting an audit of SCP-2632 as part of the investigation. The Foundation isn’t stupid, they’d connect the dots and I think Dr. Cimmerian (the character) was already onto that,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am torn about this. While it is clever, I am sorry but the SCP foundation isn't that stupid. All it takes is one person with 2 brain cells to come read this and put 2 and 2 together. Also there is not way the ethics committee would have approved "entombing" the person.

But the twist is good enough to ignore these semi-plot wholes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly? Habit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall, I liked it, but I have one slight annoyance with the plot: Why does 2632 “kill everyone he tells?” It seems like a contrivance to get his project lead replaced, and honestly I’d like it better if he outlived his project head (he is immortal) and Beth was playing a long game to get to him. (If Beth killed him to get the job, I’m annoyed that she was able to kill him in a Facility without getting nabbed by security.)

item.title: Re: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded:
+ Possible clarification of spoilers
- collapse

So while 2632 thought Jacob was buried alive and Bethany dead, Jacob was dead and Bethany was buried alive.

Actually, all the evidence points to Jacob still being alive when he was buried. That is to say, Bethany most likely killed Jacob down there, somewhere in those 120 years spent underground together.

When I realised that, I desperately tried to upvote again :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was gonna down vote for another immortal. No vote for a good ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the question is, could a woman have a 32-year-long fertility?

and the answer is, yes, most women do have that long a fertility, if you start at puberty. And in the 19th century, marrying girls that young was not unheard of.

Hell, even today it's not unheard of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A friend of mine is nearly forty years younger than his brother, he is if I recall, slightly younger than his nephew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's 32 years older than his brother. That's not too unbelievable but if you find it so, then half brother makes sense. Also given the time period the story takes place in, there's nothing too strange about the wife's age.

Basically there's nothing unbelievable about any of the ages here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
brother: half-brother
wife: there are larger age gaps than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Explain how he's like 40 years older than his brother and his wife.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need a Tale about this and I need it now.

item.title: Re: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded: Not entirely sure why you replied to this, as I wasn't explaining the story; I was explaining how the author used the SCP format to tell the story. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
want spoilers?
now you're spoiled

For instance, the "BETH" syllable in both names.

and there were originally two people in the grave, but then one of them dug their way out. And when the grave was exhumed, the corpse that the Foundation discovered was male. So Bethany is out there somewhere. And she's immortal like Douglas is, except not permanently dying of multiple sclerosis.

And Douglas completely freaked out when he saw Elizabeth and Agent Cassidy. Yes, the report says that the freakout was because Douglas saw Agent Cassidy — but Elizabeth is the one wrote the report.

And then Elizabeth sentenced Douglas to being embedded in a block of lead, totally immobile and blind and deaf for all eternity. Just like what Douglas did to Bethany.

item.title: Re: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded:
+ SPOILERS
- Hide 

So 2632, his wife Bethany, and his brother Jacob all participated in a ritual to make them immortal, invulnerable beings that could only be harmed by each other. Bethany and Jacob started having an affair, and 2632 didn't like that. He tried to drug Jacob and bury him alive, but screwed up the dosage so that Jacob died of an overdose. Bethany found out and berated 2632, who responded by hitting her in the head with a shovel and then burying her with Jacob. He thought he killed her - but he only knocked her unconscious. So while 2632 thought Jacob was buried alive and Bethany dead, Jacob was dead and Bethany was buried alive.

In the 1970s, SCP-2632 returned to the site and found that they had been dug up inadvertently, so he got scared and went into hiding, which led to him being put into the Foundation's custody. Bethany tracked him down under the disguise of Elizabeth Lane, and when she came into the room with Bill Cassidy, 2632 was frightened into insanity by her - but everybody thought that Bill was the cause. Bethany/Lane then had 2632 put into the lead press, thus burying him alive and getting her revenge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Question
- Question

I'm still confused, how would I come to the conclusion that Bethany and Elizabeth are the same person?

item.title: Re: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded: Am I stupid or something, I still don't really get this.

item.title: Re: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded: This is a good post. Speaking as somebody who has a hard time enjoying most SCP's on more than a superficial level, I'd love to see more posts like this.

item.title: What Did They Do Write? #1
item.content:encoded:
Make sure to read the article first, as Here Be Spoilers.

What is this?
– hide block

One piece of feedback we see every so often is 'read more SCP's' particularly directing newbies towards Series 2/3 articles. While this may help some people who's main issue is a fixation on Series one (and therefore are not aware of the progression in quality exhibited in later works) this doesn't help people who can't really analyze/pin down what exactly makes other mainlist articles viable.

Even going into an article's discussion page, one may only be able to find generic praise, along with a few (long since implemented) nitpicks. Oftentimes, people will give more in-depth feedback/critique to point out why something doesn't work for them. Here, I try to do it the other way around.

In "What Did They Do Write" I'm going to try to do a breakdown of a successful article. There are a ton of ways to produce such an article, so I'm going to try to do this every so often. Hopefully this will help people break down articles they enjoy, to see what makes them tick, and learn from the process.

This was inspired by this post from Aiden Eldritch, and the resulting discussion.

Without further ado:

Everybody's got something to write
'cept for me and my monkey

As of this writing, the wiki has been around for quite some time. Aside from the fact that we have 2500+ individual SCP articles, we're a community of writers. Therefore, there are a verifiable ton of ideas that have been seen time and again (and in some cases, dozens if not hundreds of times). One way to accomplish a successful article is to have a truly unique and/or different anomaly/effect.

In 2632, Cimmerian takes a different approach. Let's get into it:

SCP-2632 is to be contained in a standard humanoid containment cell in Site-88's high-value containment wing. SCP-2632 may not physically interact with any Foundation staff directly except while being restrained.

Very simple, cost effective, and most importantly for the purpose of the article; short. While I personally dig complex containment procedures with miss-'em-if-you-skim Super Important lines/hooks, this is thorough.

SCP-2632 is a human individual that possesses unusual longevity, and is unable to be harmed by any available means.
SCP-2632 displays physical characteristics which are consistent with a 68-year-old male
SCP-2632 possesses no further anomalous properties

So, right off the bat, we know what this thing is, and what it does. There aren't 200 words spent describing the color of his eyes, precise physical measurements, and favorite cereal. Clear and concise, and a quick overview of its recovery. However, as far as anomalies go, this is…about as bland as one can get, right? How can one be effective with something so straightforward?

On to the interview, the meatiest portion of the article.

Dr. Hoskins was instructed to create a rapport with the subject to induce cooperation. Those portions of the interview have been edited out for brevity.

One thing I'd like to point out here: In a single line, the author is able to place the reader right into the story with the necessary background that it and the interviewer have, and is not bogged down with a superfluous 'adjustment to captivity' as is often seen. It begins, essentially, in medias res.

The interview itself is well written. It explores the skip's backstory in an evocative manner that would have been unwieldy had it been constrained by the clinical tone that must be maintained in the main body of the article. In his own words, we're able to follow our protagonist on his journey. We also become privy to some crucial pieces of information, weaved naturally into the world-building:

Turned out there was one catch: people who'd participated could hurt each other. It was the only way you could die.
Dr. Hoskins: 120? You said you moved to Crossroads in 1867?
SCP-2632: They must've because he found me. Never saw him. Fucking coward caught me from behind.

Now, there's a reason I opened this with a comment on the wiki and its age/userbase in general: there's quite a few people here who are genre-savvy. Right about here is where I figured the brother was going to return in some way to exact his revenge. It's what I expected from being familiar with basic tropes not just from the wiki, but fiction in general.

Next collapsible, we find out the fate of the good doctor, as promised earlier, 2632 murders him. As is standard fare for such events, a quick notation of the event is applied. We get a quick mention of the new project head…with an insidious detail:

When she entered the interview chamber along with Agent Bill Cassidy, SCP-2632 began to suffer from what appeared to be a mental break.

SCP-2632 was unable to answer any questions cogently, and appeared to be unusually preoccupied with Agent Cassidy's presence

Completely reinforcing what I already expected. Everything has led up to this: the Foundation may be clueless (as usual, right?) but I know better. Clearly he's returned to exact his revenge.

a press will move a block of lead approximately SCP-2632's size downward until it molds into SCP-2632's shape.
I have some concerns with regards to your utilizing Agent Cassidy's containment expertise

…and isn't that some poetic justice there? The skip meets a tragic end, Cassidy looks to be under fire, all the loose ends are tied up. An ending most could see from a mile away. But…there's still more? An ethics committee report?

Oh. Oh fuck.

Everything up until the final collapsible is constructed in such a way as to assure that you expect the story to play out one way, right before it rips you out of that comfort zone quickly and violently.

In summary: Subvert expectations. When you write an scp, think about what it is you're writing. Is this something people might be familiar with? Have they seen this story play out? (and most importantly) Can I use the reader's expectations to my advantage?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. I didn't quite get it at first, but a nice twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So why did Cimmerian and the ethics committee go along with it? The initial argument wasn't that convincing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might very well be scip of the year for me! Ain't no horror like Gothic horror, and this is a modern minor masterpiece of the genre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. Oh fuck. What a clever twist and a great way to subtly enact it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fucking snorted laughing when I made the connection. A+.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it certainly worked out well for Elizabeth Lane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a great presentation. My only beef is that it was awfully convenient to bury them in the same grave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. That is pretty clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best thing I've read in a long time, no exaggeration. I love this. This is my new favorite skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I was unimpressed with another "immortal SCP that can't be harmed."

Then I realized what that facial reconstruction meant.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cimmerian, you clever fuck.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slick. The interview dragged a bit, but you did a damn good job building up to that reveal.

I'm sure SCP-2566 wishes he'd found out about that ritual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading this as a draft and not getting the twist and being particularly underwhelmed.

Then the twist helped the blocks fall into place, and really made the piece come together.

Overall its a really solid piece showing, yet again, that immortality sucks. This point is especially true when you can create enemies with nothing but time in which to plan your downfall.

+1 Cimmerian. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image is from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Forensic_facial_reconstruction_of_Alberto_di_Trento.jpg

Thanks to Jekeled, Conwell, TwistedGears, Expletive, JackIke, Shio, Decibelle, ARD, and Levi for the review.

+ show block
– hide block

Don't look back in anger.
I heard her say.

Spoiler below.

+ show block
– hide block

Bethany and Elizabeth are the same person.
